session_b_findings.finding: The developmental psychology framework surfaces a question not fully addressed: does goodness develop in stages within the individual inner being, and if so, what are the stage markers? The verse evidence names transformation arcs (Q&A-126) but does not give a developmental stage model. Whether the Psa 73 arc (crisis → reorientation → overflow) constitutes a stage sequence that is normative or a particular narrative instance is not resolved. This is a genuine gap — further investigation of whether the biblical evidence implies developmental stages in goodness would require a wider reading of the Psalms lament-to-praise movement and NT formation texts. This gap is identified but does not require immediate Stage 2a reopening — it is more naturally Session C or Session D territory. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-039)

What T1 named (goodness as primarily a quality — a settled characterological property) is confirmed and located by T2. The constitutional movement identified in T2 (six-step: Spirit → soul → heart → volition → agency → expression) explains how a characterological quality (T1's finding) is not a static property but a dynamic disposition that moves through the person from its deepest level outward. T1's four constituent poles (ontological, moral-character, experiential, volitional) map directly onto T2's constitutional levels: the ontological pole operates at Spirit-level origin; the moral-character pole at soul and heart; the experiential pole at soul-seeking; the volitional pole at the volitional step. The two tiers read together reveal that goodness is a quality that is simultaneously constitutionally located and constitutionally mobile — it has its deepest root at the Spirit level and its expression at the social-ethical level.

T1 identified goodness as an integrating characteristic with 12 modes of operation. T3 confirms the mechanism of integration: goodness is integrating because it engages and aligns every inner faculty simultaneously. The T1 finding that goodness has a communicative mode (doxological declaration and divine revelatory speech — Q&A-024) connects to T3's perceptual (Q&A-066) and relational (Q&A-096) faculty findings: the communicative mode of goodness engages perception (tasting and seeing), activates conscience (the divine word naming what is good calibrates the conscience's standard), and builds relational capacity (community-forming declaration). The T3 finding that goodness is fundamentally integrating (Q&A-095) is what makes its T1 multi-modality coherent: the 12 modes are not fragmented operations but facets of a single faculty-integrating reality.

T1 named goodness as carrying directional, relational, and constitutional implications in its very name (Q&A-015). T4 maps those implications onto the relational architecture: the directional implication (goodness flows from God and toward others) is precisely the God-to-human cascade confirmed in T4; the relational implication (goodness operates in relationship) is confirmed by the extensive T4 evidence across all three relational directions; the constitutional implication (goodness is a character depth, not a surface act) is confirmed by the T4 finding that human-to-human giving of goodness requires prior constitutional transformation (Q&A-109). T1's naming-level insight (Q&A-015) is validated by T4's relational-structure evidence: the name carries the architecture.

T1's distinction between immediate response and sustained effect (Q&A-030) maps precisely onto T5's two transformation sequences (Q&A-126). The immediate response pattern (trust, praise, receptive desire) corresponds to the initial stages of T5's positive arc (encounter → trust → refuge). The sustained effect (stable orientation, community capacity, settled disposition) corresponds to T5's later stages (reorientation → overflow → formation-agent capacity). Furthermore, T1's "sustained effect" (Q&A-028) identified stable inner orientation as the primary sustained product — T5 confirms this through the transformation to orientation change (Q&A-123) and the formation-agent role (Q&A-136). The two tiers read together reveal a temporal architecture for goodness: immediate contact produces first responses; sustained formation produces constitutive character; and the character-formed person becomes a formative resource for others.

T1's boundary analysis (Q&A-019-021) identified three structural opposites and named the boundary problem with adjacent characteristics as partially unresolved. T6's structural relationships data confirms and deepens this: the boundary between goodness and love (R103), kindness (R99), and generosity (R65) is structurally unresolved at the root level — the vocabulary sharing (T6.4) and co-occurrence data (T6.1) reveal that these registries are adjacent precisely because they share root families and co-anchor at the same verses. T1's definition of goodness as constituted by the Mic 6:8 tri-fold (justice + kindness + humility — Q&A-152) directly names kindness as a component of goodness — which means the goodness-kindness boundary (T1.3 partially answered, T6.5 partially answered) is not between two separate characteristics but between a whole and one of its constituents. This reading resolves the boundary partially: kindness may be a constituent mode of goodness rather than a peer characteristic. Session D testing required.

T1 identified that goodness is complex in structure — the name "goodness" does not capture the semantic breadth of the vocabulary (Q&A-013). T7 confirms and explains this complexity through the vocabulary arc (Q&A-177): the three-stage development (H2896A's phenomenological breadth → agathōsunē's virtuous precision → chrēstotēs's relational precision) corresponds precisely to T1's finding that goodness operates across quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act (Q&A-016). Each stage of the vocabulary arc covers a different aspect of T1's definitional complexity: H2896A covers quality, condition, and evaluative act; agathōsunē covers the character-quality and virtuous-resolve aspect; chrēstotēs covers the dispositional-relational aspect. T7 thus provides the lexical grounding for T1's multi-modal portrait — the vocabulary complexity matches the definitional complexity.

T2 identified the constitutional movement of goodness from Spirit-level through soul and heart to outward expression (six-step sequence, Q&A-064). T3 names the faculty operations at each level: Spirit-level corresponds to Spirit-production (T2.1) → T3.6 volitional reconstitution (the Spirit enables the resolve for good); soul-level seeking (T2.2) → T3.1 perceptual encounter (tasting and seeing); heart-level knowing and gladness (T2.3) → T3.3 memory and T3.4 affect; evaluative-cognitive function (T2.4) → T3.2 cognition and T3.8 moral evaluation; outward expression (T2.10) → T3.7 agency and T3.11 relational capacity. The two tiers together reveal that the constitutional movement is not an abstract structural description but a faculty-by-faculty account of how goodness moves through the person. Each constitutional level corresponds to specific faculty engagements. The T3 faculty map gives content to the T2 constitutional architecture.

T2 confirmed that goodness originates externally to the person — the origin is God's character, Spirit, and word; the person is the site of reception, not generation (Q&A-060-061). T4 maps the relational architecture of this origin: the God-to-human direction (T4.1) is the relational channel through which the external origin reaches the person; the human-to-God direction (T4.2) is the responsive movement that the received goodness enables; the human-to-human direction (T4.3) is the overflow once received. T2's single-origin/multiple-mechanism finding (Q&A-061) maps onto T4's three relational directions: the encounter mechanism corresponds to the human-to-God direction (seeking and tasting); the covenantal-word mechanism corresponds to the God-to-human covenantal direction; the Spirit-infusion mechanism is the Spirit's work in the human constitutional levels (T2.1-2.2). The constitutional and relational architectures are co-extensive — they describe the same reality from inside (constitutional) and outside (relational).

T2's constitutional movement (six-step sequence) and T5's transformation sequences (positive arc and moral awareness arc) describe the same developmental reality from two analytical perspectives. T2 describes where goodness moves in the person (constitutional levels); T5 describes how goodness moves the person (mechanisms and sequences). The six-step T2 sequence (Spirit → soul → heart → volition → agency → expression) is the constitutional substrate through which T5's transformation arcs operate. T5's three mechanisms (encounter, Spirit-infusion, recognition-confrontation) are the relational/contextual triggers that activate the T2 constitutional movement. Together: the T2 constitutional architecture provides the vertical structure through which T5's developmental arcs run. Formation is not a disembodied process — it is the progressive deepening of goodness through the constitutional levels identified in T2.

T2 confirmed that goodness's origin is singular (God) but its delivery is multiple (encounter, word, Spirit). T6 confirmed that goodness's vocabulary is distributed across multiple registries through shared root families. These two findings are structurally related: the vocabulary distribution (T6.4 — TOV root to R103, AGATHO root to R65, CHRESTO root to R99) reflects the multiple delivery mechanisms of T2. The TOV root's distribution to love (R103) corresponds to the encounter-relational delivery mechanism; the AGATHO root's distribution to generosity (R65) corresponds to the action-expression level of the constitutional movement; the CHRESTO root's shared presence across R67 and R99 corresponds to the dispositional-kindness register of chrēstotēs. The vocabulary distribution is not arbitrary — it tracks the constitutional and relational architecture of how goodness operates and reaches the person. The single-origin of goodness (T2.9.2) is expressed through a vocabulary distributed across the programme's relational-characteristic cluster, reflecting the multiple pathways of the one goodness.

T2's Spirit-level location finding (Q&A-037-039) — that genuine goodness requires Spirit-level intervention and is not naturally generated from within the person — is confirmed and enriched by T7's vocabulary arc (Q&A-177). The NT coinage of agathōsunē (Q&A-176) as a Spirit-fruit term reflects precisely the T2 finding: the NT needed a new term to name goodness at the Spirit-production level because the existing vocabulary (H2896A, agathos) covered the full semantic range without isolating the Spirit-produced inner virtue. The coinage is the lexical response to a constitutional reality (Spirit-level goodness as distinct from natural goodness). T7's evidential strength in lexical analysis and T2's constitutional depth together explain why the NT created agathōsunē: to name a constitutional phenomenon (Spirit-produced inner virtue at the deepest level of the person) that the existing vocabulary did not isolate with sufficient precision.

T3 confirmed that goodness engages every inner faculty (except creativity), with particular depth in moral evaluation (goodness as the normative anchor — Q&A-089), conscience (goodness calibrates the standard — Q&A-092), and relational capacity (dual-directional overflow — Q&A-098). T4 confirmed that goodness operates across all three relational directions. The inter-tier connection: the faculty engagements of T3 are the inner mechanisms through which T4's relational directions operate. The God-to-human direction (T4.1) engages the perceptive faculty (tasting and seeing — Q&A-066) and the moral evaluation faculty (calibrating the conscience's standard — Q&A-092). The human-to-God direction (T4.2) engages the affective faculty (trust, praise) and the relational capacity faculty. The human-to-human direction (T4.3) engages agency (doing justice — Q&A-084) and relational capacity (overflow toward others — Q&A-098). T3's faculty map is the inner architecture; T4's relational directions are the external expressions of those faculties in lived relationship. They are not separate dimensions but the inside and outside of the same relational-characterological phenomenon.

T3 identified three faculty engagements that are particularly significant for transformation: memory (Eze 36:31 — memory activates moral recognition, Q&A-072), affect (stable vs fragile gladness diagnostic, Q&A-077), and moral evaluation (conscience calibration, Q&A-089-090). T5's three transformation mechanisms (encounter, Spirit-infusion, recognition-confrontation — Q&A-129) map onto these faculty engagements. The recognition-confrontation mechanism (Eze 36:31 / Rom 2:4) operates specifically through the memory faculty (recall of deeds) and the conscience-moral evaluation faculty (recognition as not-good and self-loathing). The encounter mechanism operates through the perceptive faculty (tasting — Q&A-066) and the affective faculty (trust, gladness — Q&A-075). The Spirit-infusion mechanism produces faculty-level transformation: it reconstitutes the volitional faculty (Q&A-082), enables agency (Q&A-085), and generates stable affect (Q&A-076). T5's mechanisms are faculty-specific operations — each mechanism transforms the person by engaging specific faculties in specific ways. This is the most analytically precise inter-tier connection so far.

T3 confirmed that goodness is fundamentally integrating — it engages and aligns every faculty (Q&A-095). T6 confirmed that goodness is centrally connected across the programme's characteristic landscape — broad co-occurrence, shared anchors at the highest-density synthesis nodes, root-vocabulary sharing across three families. These two findings are structurally related: a characteristic that is internally integrating (T3) is also externally connecting (T6). The Mic 6:8 anchor (T6.6 — shared with 6 registries) integrates justice, kindness, love, humility, compassion, and will in a single verse — this is the structural expression of goodness's integrating faculty-engagement. Gal 5:22 (shared with 5 registries) integrates Spirit-fruit characteristics in a cluster — the same integrating logic. The characteristic that most fully integrates the inner faculties (T3) is also the characteristic most structurally connected to adjacent characteristics (T6). Integration within produces connection without.

T3's finding that goodness has an inherently perceptual character — it must be encountered and tasted, not merely inferred (Q&A-068) — is confirmed and grounded by T7's lexical and literary analysis. The Psa 34:8 primary verse functions literarily as an invitation to perceptual encounter (Q&A-178) — "taste and see" is the literary form that corresponds to the T3 perceptual faculty engagement. The phenomenological root of H2896A (Q&A-168 — begins with concrete experience of what is agreeable and beneficial, rises to moral categories) explains why T3's perceptive faculty is so prominently engaged: the Hebrew root of goodness is itself experiential-perceptual before it is ethical-conceptual. T7's etymological grounding explains the T3 perceptual finding: the vocabulary encodes the experiential mode of knowing goodness that T3's faculty analysis confirms.

T4 identified that God's goodness as a character attribute is unconditional while the human's ongoing experience of its relational benefits is conditional (Q&A-100). T5 named the transformation arcs through which the conditional dimension operates: the positive transformation arc (encounter → trust → reorientation → overflow) is the arc in which the conditional relational experience is deepened; the moral awareness arc (recognition → loathing → repentance) is the arc that recovers the relational experience when it has been lost through departure. T4's human-to-God enabling inner postures (waiting, seeking, trust — Q&A-104) correspond to T5's formation mechanisms: the posture of waiting corresponds to the encounter mechanism (sustained receptive openness); the moral awareness arc is the recovery mechanism for the person who has departed from the relational posture. Together: T5's formative processes are the mechanisms through which T4's relational conditions are entered, sustained, and restored. Transformation and relationship are inseparable in goodness.

T4 confirmed that goodness operates across all three relational directions (God-to-human, human-to-God, human-to-human). T6's structural relationships and shared anchors data confirms that the cross-registry connections of goodness are organised along the same relational axes. The Mic 6:8 shared anchor (6 co-registries) is organized along the human-to-God and human-to-human axes: humility with God (vertical) and justice/kindness with others (horizontal). The Gal 5:22 shared anchor (5 co-registries) is organized along the human-community axis: Spirit-fruit characteristics that are all expressed in human relationship. The sequential finding (goodness → repentance, goodness → faith — Q&A-147-148) corresponds to the God-to-human relational direction: divine goodness producing these inner-being responses in the human. T6's cross-registry connections are the structural record of goodness's relational reach — the registries it is most connected to are the registries it most closely interacts with in its relational operation. Relational architecture (T4) and structural position (T6) are co-extensive.

T4 confirmed that the God-to-human direction of goodness is initiatory, generous, and sustaining (Q&A-101). T7 confirms that the primary literary form carrying divine goodness (prophetic covenantal address at Mic 6:8, doxological Psalm declarations, Pauline epistolary) all use forms that emphasise divine initiative and human receptivity. T7's literary analysis (Q&A-178-179) reveals that the literary forms carrying goodness are theologically intentional: the covenant lawsuit form (Mic 6:8) establishes divine initiative in defining the good; the invitation form ("taste and see" — Psa 34:8) establishes divine initiative in the encounter; the indicative-imperative structure of Col 3:12 (God has made you chosen → therefore put on kindness) establishes divine prior action before human response. T7's literary analysis confirms T4's relational architecture through the literary forms that carry them: the literature itself models the God-initiates-human-responds structure.

T5 confirmed that goodness plays a dual developmental role — formation product and formation agent (Q&A-136). T6 confirmed that the characteristics most closely connected to goodness in co-occurrence and shared anchors are the same characteristics named in the transformation arcs. The Gal 5:22 shared anchors (love, joy, patience, peace, faith) are the characteristics that co-occur in the context of Spirit-produced formation (the fruit list). The Mic 6:8 shared anchors (justice, kindness, love, humility) are the characteristics that are co-constituted with goodness in the definition of the formed human moral life. The cross-registry connections (T6) are the structural record of what characteristics goodness produces in others when it acts as a formation agent (T5). R103 (love) as the highest co-occurrence connection (T6.1 — 28 shared verses) is also the characteristic most naturally produced by encounter with goodness in the positive transformation arc — goodness → trust and love as natural co-products. T5's formation-agent role has T6's cross-registry connections as its structural trace in the programme.

T5 named three transformation mechanisms (encounter, Spirit-infusion, recognition-confrontation) operating across three contextual registers (experiential/worship, pneumatological-community, prophetic/apostolic). T7 confirms that these three mechanisms correspond to three of the four literary forms carrying the anchor verse evidence: the encounter mechanism is carried by Psalms (experiential, invitational literature — Q&A-178-179); the Spirit-infusion mechanism is carried by NT epistolary (pneumatological instruction); the recognition-confrontation mechanism is carried by prophetic literature (covenant lawsuit and moral address). The literary form carries the transformation mechanism appropriate to it. T7's literary analysis (Q&A-179) also confirms T5's formation/sanctification finding (Q&A-135): the "fruit" metaphor of Gal 5:22 is an agricultural-developmental image embedded in a paraenetic (instructional-formative) letter — the literary form confirms that formation is the intent. T7's evidential foundation illuminates why T5's transformation mechanisms are distributed as they are: the mechanisms are embedded in literary forms designed to activate them.

T6 confirmed that goodness has the most extensive vocabulary-sharing and cross-registry connection pattern of any characteristic analysed so far in the programme. T7 confirms through the vocabulary arc (Q&A-177) that this extensiveness is a lexical reality, not a programme classification artefact: the three root families (TOV, AGATHO, CHRESTO) each generate multiple terms distributed across multiple registries because the semantic ranges genuinely overlap. The vocabulary sharing (T6.4) is not the result of how the programme has drawn its boundaries — it reflects the actual lexical distribution of the Hebrew and Greek goodness vocabulary, which crosses conceptual boundaries that English vocabulary maintains more distinctly. The three-stage vocabulary arc (H2896A breadth → agathōsunē precision → chrēstotēs precision) tracks the same movement as the cross-registry distribution pattern: H2896A generates the widest distribution (TOV root to R103, to R42, to multiple other registries); the NT precision terms (G0019, G5544) generate narrower distributions (R65 and R99 respectively). As the vocabulary narrows to precision, the cross-registry connections narrow correspondingly. T6's structural pattern and T7's lexical arc describe the same underlying phenomenon: goodness vocabulary begins broad and specialises, and its cross-registry connections follow the same trajectory.

T1 as a whole reveals that goodness is constitutively multi-modal but unified in origin. The characteristic's 12 distinct operational modes (Q&A-022) span quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act — and yet all share a single definitional structure: goodness is what proceeds from and returns to God. The immediate response varies by encounter type (Q&A-025-026), the sustained effect is constitutive rather than reactive (Q&A-030), and the dimension structure is bipartite — Moral Character for human-subject goodness, Divine-Human Correspondence for divine-subject (Q&A-034). What the tier as a whole reveals is that the apparent definitional complexity of goodness (12 modes, multiple dimensions, varied immediate responses) resolves into a coherent characterological portrait: goodness is the quality of alignment with the character of the one who is Good — variable in its expression but unified in its source and standard.

T2 as a whole reveals that goodness has a comprehensive multi-level constitutional presence — from Spirit-level origin through soul-orientation, heart-engagement, and mind-evaluative functions to outward expression — but with a marked asymmetry in evidence quality. Spirit-level (Q&A-037-039), soul-level (Q&A-040-041), and heart-level (Q&A-043-044) are all substantively evidenced. Mind-level is partially evidenced through cognitive dimensions. Body-level is thin — the gladness-of-heart compound (tov-lev) and the taste-and-see perceptual metaphor are the somatic markers, but no constitutional body deposit is evidenced (T2.8 silent). The most structurally significant T2 finding is the six-step constitutional movement (Q&A-064): Spirit-level origin → soul reorientation → heart engagement → volitional alignment → agentive resolve → outward expression. This movement is top-down and outward, with no evidenced reversal from body to inner being. Goodness is constitutionally deep but does not leave a somatic deposit.

T3 reveals that goodness is the most comprehensively faculty-engaging characteristic in the programme's current analytical record. Every faculty except creativity (T3.5 — silence confirmed) shows substantive engagement: perception (tasting and seeing goodness), cognition (moral evaluation and ranking), memory (covenantal knowing and moral reckoning), affect (stable rooted gladness vs fragile surface gladness), volition (reconstituted toward the good), agency (active expression required and enabled), moral evaluation (goodness as the normative anchor of the evaluative faculty), conscience (goodness calibrates the conscience's standard), conscientiousness (integration of all faculties into sustained moral expression), and relational capacity (dual-directional overflow). The tier as a whole reveals that goodness is not a specialist characteristic — it is an integrating one. The person who possesses genuine goodness has every faculty engaged and aligned. This comprehensive faculty-engagement is what distinguishes genuine goodness from its surface forms: surface goodness (the Haman problem) engages affect without engaging conscience or volitional alignment; genuine goodness integrates all.

T4 as a whole reveals that goodness operates across all relational directions simultaneously — God-to-human, human-to-God, and human-to-human — but with a fundamental asymmetry: the God-to-human direction is prior, generative, and abundant in evidence; the human-to-God direction is responsive and derivative; the human-to-human direction is overflow from prior reception. This is not a simple tripartite scheme but a structured cascade: God's goodness initiates and flows toward the human person (Q&A-099-101); the human responds in trust, seeking, and worship (Q&A-103-105); the goodness received overflows toward others through instruction, justice, and kindness (Q&A-107-109). The most structurally significant T4 finding is the unconditional/conditional distinction (Q&A-100): God's goodness as a character attribute is unconditional; the human's ongoing experience of its relational benefits is conditional on continued trust. This distinction has direct implications for how goodness is taught, received, and sustained. The spiritual beings interface is largely silent — the relational architecture of goodness is thoroughly human-divine in orientation.

T5 as a whole reveals that goodness is one of the most developmentally rich characteristics in the programme. Three transformation types (condition change, orientation change, character transformation — Q&A-123), two distinct transformation sequences (positive encounter arc and moral awareness arc — Q&A-126), and three context-differentiated transformation mechanisms (encounter, Spirit-infusion, recognition-confrontation — Q&A-129) together produce a portrait of a characteristic that reaches the person through multiple developmental pathways depending on their condition and context. The formation/sanctification section confirms that goodness plays a dual developmental role: it is a product of formation (Spirit-formed character) and an agent of formation (the good person enables the formation of others — Q&A-136). The relationship to suffering deepens this: suffering is not incidental but a context in which goodness is deepened, revealed, tested, and even produced through confrontation with its absence (Q&A-133). T5.7 closes formally — no somatic deposit evidenced. The eschatological trajectory is present but underdeveloped in the data (Q&A-138-139).

T6 as a whole reveals that goodness is structurally one of the most connected characteristics in the programme — both in co-occurrence breadth (top-25 co-occurrence list spanning the full dimensional spectrum), shared anchor density (7 shared anchors including the two highest-density synthesis nodes in the programme: Mic 6:8 and Gal 5:22), and root-level vocabulary sharing across three root families (TOV, AGATHO, CHRESTO) distributed across multiple registries. The structural picture confirms what T1 indicated definitionally: goodness is not a specialist characteristic but one that permeates and connects the whole inner-being landscape. The most significant T6 finding is the dual constitutive relationship (Q&A-152): goodness is simultaneously a constituent of the Spirit-fruit whole (Gal 5:22) and itself constituted by a tri-fold structure (Mic 6:8). This means goodness has both a containing relationship (it is part of something larger — Spirit-formed character) and an internal structure (it contains three orientations — justice, kindness, humility). The boundary distinctions (T6.5) remain the weakest component — all three nearest-neighbour boundaries (love, kindness, generosity) require Session D cross-registry synthesis. The R187 (strength) co-occurrence anomaly at 32 shared verses remains an open question for Session D (SP-067-027).

T7 as a whole reveals a characteristic whose evidential foundation is both exceptionally strong in some dimensions and genuinely constrained in others. Lexically: three OWNER terms across two languages with distinct semantic ranges, a full root-family analysis, a NT coinage (agathōsunē), and a comprehensive vocabulary arc from phenomenological breadth (H2896A) to virtuous precision (agathōsunē) to relational precision (chrēstotēs — Q&A-177). The lexical evidence is rich. Literary: the primary anchor (Mic 6:8) uniquely combines revelatory and covenantal-obligation frames with an internal tri-fold structure that no other verse replicates (Q&A-183); the anchor corpus spans prophetic, wisdom, psalmic, and epistolary forms across multiple contextual settings (Q&A-181). Human science: virtue ethics is the closest parallel framework, with the critical divergence at the source of goodness (divine gift vs natural achievement — Q&A-186). Constraints: NO_WORD_ANALYSIS flags on both Greek OWNER terms limit the LXX analysis (Q&A-175); LSJ data is absent. The evidential foundation is strong at the lexical-semantic and verse-literary levels; it is constrained at the LXX-continuity and human-science elaboration levels. This asymmetry is itself an analytic finding — it identifies where future investigation would most strengthen the registry's evidential base.

session_d_pointers.description: The aletheia word family spans five distinct inner-being dimensions — the widest dimensional range of any single word family reviewed in C10. This raises the question of whether 'truthfulness' is a sufficiently narrow or accurate registry label for what these terms express in their biblical usage. Session D should address the vocabulary fit and whether a broader label such as 'truth-orientation' or a restructured approach to this registry is warranted.

Registry 77 is named 'honesty' but all its terms are standard Hebrew righteousness vocabulary (tsaddiq H6662 and tsedeqah H6666). The registry label does not match its actual lexical content. Session D should address whether Registry 77 should be renamed 'righteousness' to accurately reflect its vocabulary, and how this relates to Registry 139 (righteousness, dikaioma G1345).

The programme vocabulary has no Emotional/Affective dimension. Multiple groups across C10 — particularly in innocence (930-007: anguish, dread, despair, hope) and goodness (884-003: satisfaction, longing) — describe inner states that resist classification under Moral/Conscience, Spiritual/God-ward, or Character/Disposition. Session D should assess whether the programme vocabulary requires an Emotional/Affective category to adequately map the inner-being landscape.

The eschatological transformation of the person (incorruptibility at resurrection, aftharsia G0861 in integrity Reg. 92; ashes to beauty, faint spirit to praise in faithfulness Reg. 60 group 872-007) is not adequately captured by existing dimension labels. Both groups are assigned Theological/Divine-Human as the best available fit. Session D should consider whether the programme needs a dedicated label for eschatological or transformational dimensions of the inner being.
